title: Russia launches full-scale invasion of Ukraine
summary: At 05:00 Moscow time on 24 February 2022, in a pre-dawn televised address, President Vladimir Putin announced a 'special military operation' to 'demilitarise and denazify' Ukraine. Within the hour, Russian missiles struck Kyiv, Kharkiv, Odesa and airfields across the country, and ground columns rolled south from Belarus toward the Ukrainian capital, west from the Russian border toward Kharkiv, and north out of occupied Crimea toward Kherson and Mariupol. The largest interstate war in Europe since 1945 had begun. Expected in Moscow, and by many Western intelligence services, to end in a Russian victory within days, the invasion instead ran into fierce Ukrainian resistance under President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, who refused evacuation offers with the phrase 'I need ammunition, not a ride'. The Russian advance on Kyiv was halted at Hostomel and Bucha and withdrawn by early April. Four years on, the war continues in the east and south. It has produced the largest European refugee crisis since the Second World War, driven the addition of Finland and Sweden to NATO, and reordered global energy and grain markets. Documented casualties on both sides run into the hundreds of thousands; Russian and Ukrainian civilian and military dead are the subject of continuing dispute.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Vladimir Putin, president of Russia since 2000 with a four-year interlude, ordered the full-scale invasion in the early hours of 24 February 2022.

sections.body:
The full-scale invasion of February 2022 was the culmination of an eight-year war that had begun with Russia's annexation of Crimea in March 2014 and the subsequent uprising, backed by Russian military and intelligence, in the Donetsk and Luhansk regions of the Donbas. By early 2021 the Minsk II accords, signed in February 2015, were widely regarded as a dead letter. Ukraine, under President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, elected in 2019 on a peace platform, had rearmed with Western support and moved toward closer integration with NATO and the European Union. In July 2021 Putin published a long essay entitled 'On the Historical Unity of Russians and Ukrainians' which denied that Ukraine possessed 'genuine sovereignty' and characterised the modern Ukrainian state as an artificial construct of Bolshevik nationalities policy.

From the spring of 2021 Russian forces began to accumulate on Ukraine's borders. A first buildup in April prompted alarm in Western capitals but subsided. A second, larger buildup from October 2021 involved the movement of units from as far as the Russian Far East and the deployment of some 190,000 troops around Ukraine's northern, eastern and southern flanks by mid-February 2022. In December 2021 Moscow presented Washington and NATO with two draft treaties demanding, among other things, that NATO reverse its 2008 pledge to admit Ukraine and Georgia and withdraw its forces from post-1997 members. The United States and NATO offered dialogue on arms control but rejected the core demands as non-starters.

American and British intelligence agencies took the unusual step of declassifying and publishing their assessments in the weeks before the war. President Joe Biden warned repeatedly and specifically that an invasion was imminent. Many European capitals were sceptical; Ukraine's own president publicly criticised the Anglo-American warnings as damaging to the economy. On 21 February 2022, in a lengthy televised address, Putin formally recognised the Donetsk and Luhansk People's Republics and ordered Russian troops in as 'peacekeepers'. Three days later, before dawn on 24 February, he announced the launch of a 'special military operation' whose stated goals were the 'demilitarisation and denazification' of Ukraine and the protection of ethnic Russians in the Donbas. Cruise and ballistic missiles hit targets across Ukraine within minutes; Russian ground forces crossed the border at multiple points; Russian airborne troops landed at Hostomel airport outside Kyiv.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: Volodymyr Zelenskyy, elected in 2019, remained in Kyiv through the assault and became the war's most visible symbol of resistance.

sections.body:
The Russian plan called for a rapid, multi-front decapitation of the Ukrainian state within days. The main effort was the drive on Kyiv from Belarus, spearheaded by the Vozdushno-Desantnye Voyska (VDV) airborne forces and mechanised units of the Western Military District. On 24 February Russian helicopters landed troops at Hostomel airport, twenty kilometres north-west of the capital, in an attempt to seize a bridgehead for airlifted reinforcements. Ukrainian counter-attacks by the National Guard and the 4th Rapid Reaction Brigade denied Russia use of the runway. A forty-mile column of Russian armour crawling south from the Belarusian border toward Kyiv became one of the war's early emblems: bogged down in mud, plagued by fuel and food shortages, and picked apart by Ukrainian anti-tank ambushes armed with Javelins and NLAWs. By 25 March the Russian General Staff announced that the 'first phase' of the operation was complete and forces would refocus on the Donbas. Between 30 March and 3 April Russian forces withdrew entirely from the Kyiv, Chernihiv and Sumy regions.

As Ukrainian troops returned to the reoccupied areas, they uncovered the killings at Bucha, a suburb north-west of Kyiv, where hundreds of civilian bodies were found in the streets and mass graves; the International Criminal Court subsequently issued arrest warrants for senior Russian officials in connection with these and other events. In the south the port city of Mariupol was besieged from the first days of the war. Its defenders, including the Azov Regiment, held out amid the ruins of the Azovstal steel works until 20 May 2022. Mariupol's civilian toll was catastrophic; Ukrainian and UN estimates put the dead at well over ten thousand, though a full accounting has not been possible under Russian occupation. Kharkiv, Ukraine's second city, was shelled continuously but held.

Ukrainian counter-offensives in September 2022, particularly the rapid Kharkiv operation that recovered some 12,000 square kilometres in under two weeks, and the November 2022 recovery of Kherson city on the west bank of the Dnipro, marked the high point of Ukrainian battlefield success. A subsequent Ukrainian offensive in the summer of 2023 in Zaporizhzhia oblast, launched against dense Russian minefields and prepared defensive lines, made only limited territorial gains. From late 2023 Russia recovered the initiative, taking the shattered city of Bakhmut in May 2023 after a nine-month battle in which the Wagner private military company played a leading role, Avdiivka in February 2024, and a series of smaller towns across 2024 and 2025. Western military assistance, most notably HIMARS multiple-launch rocket systems from June 2022, Patriot and IRIS-T air defence, Leopard 2 and M1 Abrams main battle tanks from 2023, F-16 fighter aircraft from mid-2024, and ATACMS long-range missiles, kept Ukrainian forces in the fight.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Aftermath of the Russian missile strike on central Kyiv, 10 October 2022, which killed five civilians and marked the start of the systematic campaign against Ukrainian energy infrastructure.

sections.body:
The international response was immediate and, in relative terms, unprecedented. On 2 March 2022 the UN General Assembly, meeting in emergency session, adopted Resolution ES-11/1 deploring the invasion and demanding a full Russian withdrawal by 141 votes to 5, with 35 abstentions. Only Belarus, North Korea, Eritrea, Syria and Russia itself voted no. The European Union, United States, United Kingdom, Canada, Japan, Australia and others imposed successive rounds of sanctions targeting the Russian central bank's foreign reserves (some $300 billion of which was frozen), major Russian state banks, hundreds of individuals close to Putin, and eventually much of the energy trade. The Nord Stream pipelines connecting Russian gas to Germany were sabotaged in unclear circumstances in September 2022. Germany, in a policy reversal signalled by Chancellor Olaf Scholz's 27 February 2022 Zeitenwende (turning point) speech, committed to raising defence spending above the NATO 2% target and to arming Ukraine. Finland and Sweden, both formally neutral since the Second World War, applied to join NATO within weeks; Finland acceded in April 2023 and Sweden in March 2024.

The human costs were staggering. The UN High Commissioner for Refugees recorded more than eight million Ukrainians fleeing to European countries in the first year, the largest refugee movement on the continent since 1945. Millions more were internally displaced. UN OCHA has documented more than ten thousand civilian deaths in Ukraine and tens of thousands of civilian injuries, with the true toll believed to be significantly higher in areas beyond the reach of monitors. Military casualties have been officially opaque on both sides; leaked American intelligence estimates in 2023 suggested Russian dead at more than 100,000 and Ukrainian dead in the tens of thousands, with combined casualties over 500,000, and the figures have continued to climb.

The war has also had large second-order effects on the wider world. Ukraine and Russia together supplied roughly a quarter of global wheat and much of the sunflower oil trade before 2022; disruptions and Black Sea shipping restrictions drove sharp increases in global food prices in 2022 that hit hardest in Africa and the Middle East. European gas prices spiked and only slowly normalised. China, India, Turkey and the United Arab Emirates became key alternative destinations for Russian oil sold at a discount. Iran and North Korea emerged as significant Russian arms suppliers, with Iranian-designed Shahed drones and North Korean artillery shells playing major roles in the Russian war effort.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Even as fighting continues, the strategic consequences of the 2022 invasion are already substantial. NATO, an alliance that French President Emmanuel Macron in 2019 had described as 'brain dead', has enlarged, rearmed, and refocused on collective defence in Europe. Poland has become the alliance's largest per-capita defence spender and one of the most important suppliers of aid to Ukraine. Germany has taken steps, however halting, toward re-becoming a serious military power. The United States, whose long-term strategic focus was shifting to China, has been drawn deeply back into European security.

Inside Russia the war has been accompanied by the near-total suppression of independent media, the criminalisation of the words 'war' and 'invasion' in reference to the fighting, and the exile or imprisonment of most remaining opposition figures. The opposition leader Alexei Navalny died in an Arctic penal colony on 16 February 2024. The private military entrepreneur Yevgeny Prigozhin, whose Wagner Group had spearheaded the taking of Bakhmut, marched on Moscow in an armed mutiny on 24 June 2023, only to be killed two months later when his plane crashed in circumstances universally attributed to Russian security services. In September 2022 Putin announced a 'partial mobilisation' of 300,000 reservists that prompted the flight of hundreds of thousands of Russian men across the borders to Georgia, Kazakhstan, Armenia and Turkey.

Ukraine has emerged as, in Zelenskyy's phrasing, 'the shield of Europe'. Its armed forces have adapted, iterated and innovated at speed, pioneering the mass battlefield use of small drones and demonstrating the effectiveness of unmanned surface vessels against Russian warships in the Black Sea. The country's application for European Union membership was accepted in June 2022; accession negotiations opened in June 2024. The war has also imposed enormous costs, human and material, on Ukraine, and the terms on which it will eventually end - by negotiated settlement, ceasefire, or continued attrition - remain, at this writing in mid-2026, unresolved.

figures.name: Vladimir Putin
figures.role: President of the Russian Federation; ordered the invasion in his televised address of 24 February 2022 and has directed the war since

figures.name: Volodymyr Zelenskyy
figures.role: President of Ukraine since May 2019; his refusal to leave Kyiv in the opening days of the war made him the war's most visible political figure worldwide

figures.name: Valerii Zaluzhnyi
figures.role: Ukrainian commander-in-chief from July 2021 to February 2024, credited with the defence of Kyiv and the Kharkiv counter-offensive of September 2022

figures.name: Sergei Shoigu
figures.role: Russian defence minister at the time of the invasion, removed and reassigned as Security Council secretary in May 2024

figures.name: Yevgeny Prigozhin
figures.role: Head of the Wagner Group private military company whose forces led the battle for Bakhmut; killed in a plane crash on 23 August 2023, two months after his abortive mutiny

images.caption: Destroyed apartment blocks in the Livoberezhnyi District of Mariupol, photographed by the Mariupol City Council in May 2022 during the Russian siege.
Map of the Russian invasion of Ukraine showing territorial control, updated repeatedly since 24 February 2022.
